description: A diagram illustrating the FP8 floating point format, showing the bit-level breakdown of sign, exponent, and mantissa fields for both E4M3 and E5M2 variants, with labeled bit positions and their roles in representing floating point numbers.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize how reducing bit-width from 32 to 8 affects the allocation between exponent and mantissa bits, and understand the trade-off between representable range (E5M2) and precision (E4M3) in quantized formats.
when_to_show: Show when introducing quantization formats, specifically when explaining why different FP8 variants (E4M3 vs E5M2) exist and how bit allocation determines representable range (-448 to 448 for E4M3) versus precision.
suggested_caption: FP8 bit layout: E4M3 vs E5M2 formats showing sign, exponent, and mantissa. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: An animated GIF illustrating the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) mechanism, showing how small trainable adapter matrices are added on top of a frozen base model, with the animation likely depicting the forward pass through frozen weights and the parallel low-rank update path.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how LoRA enables training of large quantized models by keeping base model weights frozen and only updating small adapter matrices, making it intuitive why this approach is memory-efficient for 4-bit/8-bit models.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how 4-bit or 8-bit quantized models can still be fine-tuned, particularly when discussing RLHF use cases where multiple adapters (reward model, value policy) are trained on a single quantized base model.
suggested_caption: LoRA adapters enable fine-tuning of frozen 4-bit quantized base models. (HuggingFace Blog)

description: A PCA/SVD visualization showing how a high-dimensional dataset can be approximated by a lower-dimensional representation, illustrating the conceptual foundation of low-rank decomposition with axes and projected data points.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract concept of low-rank approximation in the familiar PCA framework, helping students see that LoRA is an application of a well-known dimensionality reduction principle to weight matrices.
when_to_show: Introduce this before explaining LoRA math — use it to build intuition that high-dimensional data/matrices often have a lower-dimensional structure that captures most information.
suggested_caption: PCA illustrates low-rank approximation: the core idea behind LoRA. (Raschka, 2023)

description: Diagram of standard full finetuning showing a weight matrix W being updated to W' = W + ΔW, with input x and output h, illustrating how the full weight update matrix is applied during conventional finetuning.
teaching_value: Establishes the baseline formulation W' = W + ΔW so students understand what LoRA is replacing, making the efficiency gain concrete by contrast.
when_to_show: Show before introducing LoRA decomposition — students need to understand the standard update rule to appreciate why decomposing ΔW saves parameters.
suggested_caption: Standard finetuning updates full weight matrix W with ΔW. (Raschka, 2023)

alt: Llm finetuning lora regular finetuning alt
description: Alternative formulation diagram showing h = Wx + ΔWx, where the original frozen weight W and the update ΔW are kept separate and both applied to input x, with outputs summed.
teaching_value: Introduces the additive decomposition of the forward pass (h = Wx + ΔWx) that directly motivates the LoRA architecture, showing students why keeping ΔW separate is useful.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the standard finetuning diagram, as a bridge to LoRA — emphasize that this separation is what allows ΔW to be replaced by a low-rank product.
suggested_caption: Separating ΔW from W enables the LoRA low-rank substitution. (Raschka, 2023)

description: Core LoRA architecture diagram showing the original frozen weight matrix W alongside two small trainable matrices W_A (A×r) and W_B (r×B) whose product approximates ΔW, with rank r much smaller than A or B.
teaching_value: Directly visualizes the LoRA parameter reduction: instead of training an A×B matrix, only two small matrices totaling r(A+B) parameters are trained, making the efficiency gain immediately visible.
when_to_show: This is the central diagram — show it when formally introducing LoRA after the motivation diagrams, and return to it when discussing parameter counts and storage savings.
suggested_caption: LoRA decomposes ΔW into two small matrices W_A and W_B. (Raschka, 2023)

description: Annotated table from the original LoRA paper comparing ROUGE scores of LoRA against full finetuning, adapter methods, prompt tuning, and prefix tuning across multiple NLP benchmarks, showing LoRA often matches or exceeds full finetuning.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for LoRA's effectiveness, teaching students to read benchmark comparison tables and understand that parameter efficiency does not necessarily sacrifice model quality.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the LoRA mechanism, when students ask 'but does it actually work?' — use it to discuss trade-offs between efficiency and performance.
suggested_caption: LoRA matches or beats full finetuning on NLP benchmarks. (LoRA paper, via Raschka 2023)

description: Example of the Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format, showing the structure of a training example with instruction, input, and output fields used for instruction finetuning of LLaMA.
teaching_value: Concretizes what instruction-finetuning data looks like, helping students understand the input format that LoRA-finetuned models are trained on and how it differs from pretraining data.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the computational benchmarks section, before discussing training results — students need to understand the data format to contextualize the experiments.
suggested_caption: Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format used for LoRA-LLaMA finetuning. (Raschka, 2023)

description: Four-step Self-Instruct pipeline diagram showing how instruction-following datasets are automatically generated: (1) seed tasks, (2) instruction generation via LLM, (3) instance generation, (4) filtering and post-processing.
teaching_value: Explains the automated data generation pipeline behind Alpaca 52k, teaching students how large instruction datasets can be bootstrapped from a small seed set using an existing LLM.
when_to_show: Show when students ask where the Alpaca training data comes from, or when discussing data efficiency and the cost of creating instruction-following datasets.
suggested_caption: Self-Instruct 4-step pipeline generates instruction data automatically. (Raschka, 2023)

description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how multi-head attention parallelizes attention across subspaces, helping students understand why splitting into heads improves representational capacity over single attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, especially to contrast single-head vs multi-head attention and explain the concatenation + projection step.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.
teaching_value: Provides a complete structural overview of the Transformer, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact, where each component sits, and how information flows from input to output.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson or when transitioning from individual components to the full model, as a reference map students can return to.
suggested_caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions, with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white).
teaching_value: Makes the abstract sinusoidal encoding formula concrete by showing the actual pattern it produces, helping students understand how position information is encoded as unique frequency patterns across dimensions.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the positional encoding formula to give students an intuition for what the encoding looks like and why different positions produce distinguishable patterns.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.

description: Visual illustration of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position index.
teaching_value: Clarifies the geometric intuition behind RoPE — that relative position is encoded as a rotation — helping students understand why it generalizes better than additive positional encodings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching relative positional encodings, especially after sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs rotational approaches and explain RoPE's advantages for long-context tasks.
suggested_caption: RoPE encodes position as rotation in 2D subspaces of the embedding. (Su et al., 2021)

description: Side-by-side comparison of training phases for vanilla Transformer (left) and Transformer-XL (right) with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.
teaching_value: Directly illustrates the key innovation of Transformer-XL — segment-level recurrence — by contrasting it with the fixed-context vanilla Transformer, making the concept of extended attention span tangible.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and how Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism solves it.
suggested_caption: Transformer-XL caches prior segment states to extend attention span. (Dai et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory types: regular memory (recent past activations) and compressed memory (older activations compressed by a compression function), with attention attending to both.
teaching_value: Illustrates the two-tier memory hierarchy of the Compressive Transformer, helping students understand how compression extends temporal range beyond Transformer-XL while managing memory cost.
when_to_show: Show after Transformer-XL to demonstrate how compressive memory further extends context, and when discussing tradeoffs between memory size and temporal range.
suggested_caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right) in the Compressive Transformer, demonstrating that compressed memory receives meaningful attention despite being older.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that compressed memory is actually attended to, validating the design choice and helping students understand that older compressed representations retain useful information.
when_to_show: Show when discussing whether compression loses important information, as empirical evidence that the model attends to compressed memory and it is not wasted.
suggested_caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of SPALM architecture showing how short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL-style cache) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce the final output.
teaching_value: Illustrates the distinction between short-term recurrent memory and long-term external memory, and how a gating mechanism integrates both, helping students understand hybrid memory architectures.
when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, especially to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and explain how retrieval-augmented memory works.
suggested_caption: SPALM combines recurrent short-term and k-NN long-term memory via gating. (Yogatama et al., 2021)

description: Line chart comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical efficiency of fine-tuning with k-NN memory augmentation versus training from scratch, helping students understand the cost-benefit tradeoff of adding external memory to pretrained models.
when_to_show: Show when discussing practical deployment of memory-augmented Transformers, particularly to motivate fine-tuning as a cheaper alternative to full retraining.
suggested_caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches scratch-trained Memorizing Transformer performance. (Wu et al., 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) showing how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.
teaching_value: Makes the ALiBi mechanism concrete by showing exactly where and how the distance-based bias is applied to attention scores, helping students understand how it encodes relative position without learned embeddings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, especially to contrast with RoPE and learned positional encodings, and when discussing context length extrapolation strategies.
suggested_caption: ALiBi adds a negative linear distance bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)

description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the extrapolation problem in positional encodings — students can directly compare how different schemes degrade when inference context exceeds training context, making ALiBi's advantage concrete and measurable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
suggested_caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)

description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key architectural innovation of Universal Transformer — that depth is dynamic and recurrent rather than fixed, helping students distinguish it from standard Transformers and understand how RNN-style recurrence is integrated.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.
teaching_value: Shows students how the Universal Transformer adapts the standard encoder-decoder architecture by sharing recurrent weights, and highlights the cross-attention connection from decoder to encoder's final state.
when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.
teaching_value: Makes the mathematical definition of the soft mask concrete — students can see how z shifts the transition point and how the function smoothly interpolates between full attention and no attention, enabling gradient-based learning of span length.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
suggested_caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)

description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design space of early-exit strategies in transformers — the visual contrast between sequence-level and token-level granularity makes the trade-off between efficiency and flexibility immediately apparent.
when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
suggested_caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)

description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how local attention windows are defined differently for 1D (raster-scan linearized) vs 2D (spatial grid) image representations, helping students understand the trade-off between computational cost and spatial locality.
when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
suggested_caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)

description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.
teaching_value: Directly visualizes the sparsity patterns that make Sparse Transformer efficient — students can see exactly which connections are retained vs. dropped and how strided vs. fixed patterns differ, linking the abstract math to concrete matrix structure.
when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
suggested_caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)

description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).
teaching_value: Enables direct visual comparison of three influential efficient attention architectures, making it easy to see what each adds (e.g., Big Bird adds random attention on top of Longformer's local+global) and how they relate to each other.
when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
suggested_caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.

description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.
teaching_value: Shows the resulting sparse structure of LSH attention after hashing, helping students understand why only within-bucket attention is computed and how the block-diagonal pattern approximates full attention for the most relevant token pairs.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
suggested_caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.
teaching_value: Breaks down the LSH attention algorithm into discrete, visually trackable steps, allowing students to follow how a query token ends up attending only to its nearest neighbors without exhaustive pairwise comparison.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
suggested_caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how adding two projection matrices reduces the quadratic attention bottleneck to linear complexity, and the right panel provides empirical evidence of the speedup at long sequence lengths — making the theoretical claim concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Linformer after explaining the quadratic cost of standard attention; use the right panel to motivate why linear approximations matter at scale (e.g., L > 1024).
suggested_caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear attention complexity. (Wang et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computes the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reorders operations using kernel approximation φ(·), computing key-value products first to avoid the quadratic matrix, enabling linear-time attention.
teaching_value: Illustrates the core trick of RFA — that by approximating exp(x·y) with random features, the associativity of matrix multiplication can be exploited to bypass materializing the full attention matrix, making the computational savings intuitive rather than purely algebraic.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the RFA kernel approximation formula φ(x)·φ(y) ≈ exp(x·y); use it to explain why reordering (K^T V first) is valid and how it achieves linear complexity.
suggested_caption: RFA reorders attention computation via kernel features, avoiding the quadratic attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)

description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error for Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing lower variance with orthogonal sampling; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower approximation error for softmax kernel estimation.
teaching_value: Provides empirical justification for two key design choices in Performer — using orthogonal rather than i.i.d. features, and using positive rather than trigonometric features — helping students understand why these choices reduce approximation error and improve stability.
when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ after explaining the general random feature approach; use to justify why orthogonal and positive features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
suggested_caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer's FAVOR+. (Choromanski et al. 2021)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm before residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using the reordered layer norm, enabling stable training for RL.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the two structural modifications (layer norm reordering and gating units) that GTrXL introduces over Transformer-XL, helping students understand how architectural changes address training instability in the RL setting.
when_to_show: Show when introducing GTrXL as a solution to Transformer training instability in RL; use to highlight the specific architectural differences (gate placement, norm reordering) before discussing empirical RL results.
suggested_caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize Transformer training in RL. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
